elements.sent-2411-001: The majority of genetic variants identified in genome-wide association studies of complex traits are non-coding, and characterizing their function remains an important challenge in human genetics.
elements.sent-2411-002: Genomic deep learning models have emerged as a promising approach to enable in silico prediction of variant effects.
elements.sent-2411-003: These include supervised sequence-to-activity models, which predict genome-wide chromatin states or gene expression levels directly from DNA sequence, and self-supervised genomic language models.
elements.sent-2411-004: Here, we review progress in leveraging these models for non-coding variant effect prediction.
elements.sent-2411-005: We describe practical considerations for making such predictions and categorize the types of ground truth data that have been used to evaluate deep learning-based variant effect predictions, providing insight into the settings in which current models are most useful.
elements.sent-2411-006: We also discuss downstream applications of such models to understanding disease-relevant non-coding variants.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our review highlights key considerations for practitioners and opportunities for future improvements in model development and evaluation.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figure_1.png} \caption{Overview of two popular genomic deep learning modeling paradigms. a) Supervised sequence-to-activity models are trained to predict functional genomics readouts---such as chromatin accessibility, transcription factor binding, histone modifications, or gene expression---from one-hot encoded DNA sequence. These models are often trained using multi-task learning to predict multiple outputs across different cell types and assays from a single sequence, but single-task models are also common. See \ref{sec:box1} for more details. b) Self-supervised genomic language models are typically trained to predict masked portions of the input DNA sequence from the surrounding unmasked sequence context. Language models often use $k$-mer tokenization schemes to represent subsequences of DNA rather than individual nucleotides, akin to natural language models that operate at the level of words rather than individual letters. See \ref{sec:box2} for more details.} \label{fig:multitask}
elements.sent-2411-008: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified many thousands of non-coding variants associated with complex traits and diseases.
elements.sent-2411-009: Interpreting these associations requires disentangling causal variants from non-causal variants in linkage disequilibrium (LD) and identifying the genes, cell types, and tissues affected by these causal variants.
elements.sent-2411-010: Functional genomics data---such as measurements of gene expression or chromatin accessibility---can identify disease-relevant cell types in which GWAS variants are overrepresented within active genomic regions \cite{cano2020gwas .}
elements.sent-2411-011: In parallel, machine learning has become a powerful tool for modeling and extracting insights from genomics datasets.
elements.sent-2411-012: Machine learning approaches to predict the pathogenicity of protein-coding variants have been highly successful \cite{adzhubei2010method, ioannidis2016revel, cheng2023accurate, pejaver2022calibration , and similar approaches for non-coding variants---of which early examples include CADD \cite{kircher2014general and $k$-mer based models \cite{Ghandi2014,lee2015method ---have also shown promise \cite{rojano2019regulatory,ellingford2022recommendations , though interpreting non-coding variants remains a challenge.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: In this review, we focus on sequence-based deep learning approaches, adapted from computer vision and natural language processing, which have been recently successful at modeling genomic sequences and functional genomics data \cite{Zou2018, Eraslan2019, Angermuller2016, sokolova2024deep, sasse2024unlocking, consens2023transformers, benegas2024LMreview .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Two modeling paradigms are commonly used (Fig. \ref{fig:multitask ).}
elements.sent-2411-015: Supervised sequence-to-activity models \cite{Alipanahi2015, Zhou2015, quang2016danq, Kelley2016, Zeng2017cpgenie, Zhou2018, Kelley2018, wang2018define, Leung2018, Jaganathan2019, Hoffman2019deepfigv, Cheng2019, Bogard2019, Arefeen2019, VainbergSlutskin2019, Maslova2020, Kelley2020, Agarwal2020, Fudenberg2020, Schwessinger2020, minnoye2020cross, Avsec2021a, Avsec2021b, Cheng2021, Li2021, Chen2022, Zeng2022, Linder2022, Agarwal2022, Zhou2022, PampariChrombpnet, Linder2023, celaj2023bigrna, Dudnyk2024, he2024dissection, Cochran2024, Lal2024 learn from functional genomics data to predict functional activity levels from DNA sequences (Fig. \ref{fig:multitask a, \ref{sec:box1 ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Briefly, these models are trained to map from a binarized representation of a genomic DNA sequence (one-hot encoding) to output label(s) constructed from genome-wide experimental assays, including DNase-seq (DNase I hypersensitive sites sequencing) or ATAC-seq (Assay for Transposase-Accessible Chromatin with sequencing) for chromatin accessibility, ChIP-seq (Chromatin Immunoprecipitation sequencing) for transcription factor binding or histone modifications, and CAGE (Cap Analysis of Gene Expression) or RNA-sequencing for gene expression.
elements.sent-2411-017: These functional genomics data have been collected from a wide range of cell and tissue types.
elements.sent-2411-018: Prediction tasks derived from these data may be structured as binary classification (e.g. predicting the presence or absence of a called peak) or regression (predicting a continuous-valued level of activity, e.g. read count).
elements.sent-2411-019: A model can be trained to predict a single experimental assay (single-task learning) or can produce multiple predictions for a given input sequence, one for each experimental label (multi-task learning).
elements.sent-2411-020: The intuition motivating multi-task models is that sharing model weights across multiple prediction tasks with shared regulatory syntax can make it easier to learn appropriate weights.
elements.sent-2411-021: An alternative modeling approach is that of self-supervised genomic language models (Fig. \ref{fig:multitask b, \ref{sec:box2 ) \cite{consens2023transformers, benegas2024LMreview .}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: These models use self-supervised tasks adapted from natural language processing---for example, learning to predict a masked portion of the input DNA sequence from the surrounding sequence context---to construct representations of DNA sequences without knowledge of functional genomics data \cite{Zaheer2020, Ji2021, Yang2022, Zhou2023, Dalla-Torre2023, Benegas2023a, Nguyen2023, Fishman2023, Gndz2023, robson2023guanine, sanabria2023grover, zhang2023dnagpt, schiff2024caduceus, Chen2024, chu2024utrLM, zhu2024CDGPT, McNally2024allspice .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Although some supervised models also borrow architectures from language modeling \cite{Vaswani2017 , here we consider genomic language models to be those that explicitly utilize self-supervised pre-training on DNA sequence alone.}
elements.sent-2411-024: While neither modeling approach relies on data about genetic variation during training, both sequence-to-activity models and genomic language models can be used to make predictions about non-coding variant effects in a zero-shot manner (i.e. without being directly trained on the variant effect prediction task of interest).
elements.sent-2411-025: Both types of models can also be tailored to specific variant effect prediction tasks through further training on task-specific data, such as expression quantitative trait loci (expression QTLs) or GWAS data.
elements.sent-2411-026: Here, we provide a comprehensive review of how genomic deep learning models can be leveraged for in silico non-coding variant effect prediction.
elements.sent-2411-027: This review is intended as a resource for both the clinical variant interpretation and the machine learning for genomics communities.
elements.sent-2411-028: Section \ref{sec:makingVEPs describes practical considerations for using genomic deep learning models to make predictions about the effects of non-coding variants.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Section \ref{sec:evalVEP reviews evaluations that have been performed to date on genomic deep learning models when applied to various variant effect prediction tasks---such as predicting variant effects on chromatin state, gene expression levels, post-transcriptional modifications, or disease---highlighting strengths and limitations of current models and the need for more comprehensive evaluation.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Finally, Section \ref{sec:apps summarizes recent downstream applications of genomic deep learning models for interpreting disease-relevant variants.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figure_2.png} \caption{Constructing variant effect predictions using genomic deep learning models. a) For supervised models that make predictions across many cell types and assays, differences between predictions for the reference and alternate-containing alleles can be aggregated in multiple ways. In some cases, predictions from a particular cell type or task may be most relevant; in other cases, predictions can be aggregated by averaging or taking the maximum across tasks of the difference (or other distance metric) between the allele-specific predictions. b) For self-supervised genomic language models, zero-shot variant effect predictions can be made by masking the variant position of interest and comparing the model's predicted probabilities for the reference and alternate alleles using a specific distance metric.} \label{fig:vep_scoring}
elements.sent-2411-032: Once trained, both supervised and self-supervised genomic deep learning models can make predictions for any DNA sequence of the appropriate input size.
elements.sent-2411-033: To make an effect prediction for any variant of interest using a supervised model, two separate predictions---one for the sequence containing the reference allele and one for the alternate allele---can be generated and compared (Fig. \ref{fig:vep_scoring a).}
elements.sent-2411-034: For self-supervised language models, predicted nucleotide likelihood probabilities at the location of a variant of interest can be compared (Fig. \ref{fig:vep_scoring b).}
elements.sent-2411-035: For language models that have been additionally trained or fine-tuned on functional genomics data, variant effect predictions can be made as they would be for supervised models.
elements.sent-2411-036: In this section, we describe key considerations when constructing variant effect predictions using both types of models.. Basic considerations.
elements.sent-2411-037: A number of practical considerations are important when constructing variant effect predictions from genomic deep learning models.
elements.sent-2411-038: For example, different distance metrics can be used to compare the allele-specific predictions from supervised models, including absolute or relative difference between reference and alternate predictions.
elements.sent-2411-039: Both absolute and relative differences have been shown to be informative for variant effect prediction \cite{Zhou2018, Avsec2021a , but no published work has directly compared them.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Another consideration is where along a genomic sequence to predict the effect of a variant.
elements.sent-2411-041: For example, a variant might have a proximal effect on chromatin state and also modulate expression of a distal gene.
elements.sent-2411-042: Effect predictions can be computed at the genomic location of the variant itself, at a gene start site or other site of interest (if the distance to the variant is less than the input context size of the model), or aggregated over a larger genomic region.
elements.sent-2411-043: The input sequence can also be centered at either the variant or another site such as a gene start site.
elements.sent-2411-044: These and other practical choices for variant effect prediction with genomic deep learning models have not yet been systematically compared.
elements.sent-2411-045: Aggregating predictions across multiple cell or tissue types.
elements.sent-2411-046: Many supervised models use multi-task learning to make simultaneous predictions for hundreds or thousands of cell types and experimental assays.
elements.sent-2411-047: Variant effects can be predicted for each task, and when multiple tasks are relevant, the predictions can be aggregated into a single score (Fig. \ref{fig:vep_scoring a).}
elements.sent-2411-048: Aggregation may involve averaging the variant effect predictions from many cell types or assays \cite{Kelley2016 , taking the maximum variant effect prediction \cite{Dey2020 , utilizing a global, lower-dimensional representation of the variant effect predictions \cite{Chen2022 , or training an additional supervised model (e.g. the probing approach in \ref{sec:box3 ) to learn an appropriate weighting over the variant effect predictions for a downstream task \cite{Zhou2015, Kelley2018, Avsec2021b .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Extracting zero-shot variant effect predictions from self-supervised genomic language models.
elements.sent-2411-050: Variant effect predictions can be made directly (zero-shot) from a model pre-trained on a self-supervised task such as masked language modeling (\ref{sec:box2 ), or made after additional training of a pre-trained model on supervised data (\ref{sec:box3 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-051: To make zero-shot predictions, the variant location is typically masked within the input sequence and the predicted probability of each nucleotide (or token) being found at that masked position (summing to 1) is generated from the model (Fig. \ref{fig:vep_scoring b).}
elements.sent-2411-052: To compare the predicted probabilities for the variant's reference and alternate alleles (or allele-containing tokens), one can use the log-likelihood ratio \cite{Benegas2023, Benegas2023a or other distance metrics \cite{Dalla-Torre2023, Chen2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Different choices of metric make it difficult to compare the reported performance of existing models.
elements.section-2411-006: Evaluating variant effect predictions on molecular and organismal phenotypes
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Table_1.png} \caption*{Table 1: Overview of variant effect prediction evaluations that have been performed to date using current genomic deep learning models. Evaluations performed either in the original modeling papers or in independent studies are indicated. Data used for evaluation are grouped into four broad categories: epigenetic or chromatin-level phenotypes (yellow), transcription or gene expression phenotypes (blue), post-transcriptional phenotypes (red), and organismal trait or disease-related phenotypes (purple). } \label{fig:vep_tab1}
elements.sent-2411-054: The effect of a variant can be observed at multiple scales, including its effect on epigenetic and chromatin states, gene expression, post-transcriptional regulation, and trait and disease (Fig. \ref{fig:vep ).}
elements.sent-2411-055: Successful variant effect prediction at a given scale may require specific modeling choices.
elements.sent-2411-056: For example, chromatin states are thought to be primarily modulated by proximal sequence \cite{degner2012dnase , while gene expression can be regulated by distal enhancers \cite{Gasperini2020 .}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Variant effects on such phenotypes may also be specific to a particular cell type or cell state, while variant effects on organism-level traits and diseases are often mediated by a particular cell type, but the relevant cell type may be unknown.
elements.sent-2411-058: In this section, we review how variant effect predictions from genomic deep learning models have been evaluated at each scale, including the relevant types of ground truth data.
elements.sent-2411-059: We include evaluations performed in the original modeling papers as well as those performed by external benchmarking studies.
elements.sent-2411-060: Table 1 provides an overview of the variant effect prediction evaluations that have been performed to date for existing genomic deep learning models.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figure_3.png} \caption{Variant effects at multiple scales, with diverse ground truth data. The effect of a variant can be measured on a range of phenotypes (left), including epigenetic or chromatin states, gene expression levels (transcription), post-transcriptional modifications, and disease or complex traits. Different types of experimental or observational data (right) can be used as ground truth data to evaluate variant effect prediction accuracy for each of these phenotypes. } \label{fig:vep}
elements.subsection-2411-001: Predicting variant effects on epigenetic or chromatin state
elements.sent-2411-061: Local epigenetic or chromatin state includes the presence of DNA methylation, histone modifications, transcription factor (TF) binding, and accessible chromatin.
elements.sent-2411-062: A model that can correctly predict the effect of a variant on these phenotypes is likely to have learned salient patterns of local regulatory syntax, such as relevant TF binding motifs, as well as the functional effects of disrupting those patterns.
elements.sent-2411-063: Note that predicting the effect of a variant, which requires a causal understanding of the relevant sequence features, tends to be more challenging than annotating chromatin states within endogenous sequences, which involves recognizing sequence features that are characteristic of particular states even when non-causal.
elements.sent-2411-064: Similarly, \citet{Hoffman2019deepfigv found that variants with high effect predictions from their DeepFIGV model are enriched among chromatin accessibility QTLs.}
elements.sent-2411-065: DeepSEA, DeepFIGV, DeepBind \cite{Alipanahi2015 , Enformer \cite{Avsec2021b , and Sei \cite{Chen2022 have also been evaluated on classifying allele-specific TF binding variants from ChIP-seq data and have moderate performance, with substantial room for improvement \cite{Wagih2018, Han2024, Hoffman2019deepfigv .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Finally, several supervised models trained to predict 3D chromatin structure were shown by their authors to correctly predict the effects of a small number of experimentally validated structural variants on genome structure \cite{Fudenberg2020, Schwessinger2020, Trieu2020deepmilo, Zhou2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-067: Overall, however, in comparison to gene expression (discussed below), relatively few models have been evaluated on their ability to predict variant effects on epigenetic or chromatin state, despite often using many such tracks during training.
elements.sent-2411-068: This may be due to the greater availability of ground truth datasets for variant effects on gene expression.
elements.sent-2411-069: However, because variant effects on downstream phenotypes such as gene expression and complex traits can be mediated by changes in local epigenetic or chromatin state, we believe additional evaluation of models on such local variant effects is crucial to inform how they can best be utilized and improved.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Predicting variant effects on gene expression
elements.sent-2411-070: Gene expression is a commonly used phenotype for evaluating variant effect predictions from genomic deep learning models.
elements.sent-2411-071: Observational studies and synthetic perturbation experiments are the primary sources of data on expression-altering variants used in these evaluations, and each has important advantages and limitations.
elements.sent-2411-072: Observational studies reflect variant effects in their native chromatin context, but are typically powered to detect the effects of only relatively common variants.
elements.sent-2411-073: They also contain both non-causal and causal variant associations (due to LD) and may be biased by the genetic ancestry of the included individuals.
elements.sent-2411-074: Experimental assays, such as massively parallel reporter assays (MPRAs) \cite{Tewhey2016, Kircher2019 and CRISPR-based perturbation experiments \cite{Gasperini2019, Fulco2019 , can test expression from synthetic DNA sequences or make perturbations to native DNA, enabling the collection of functional data from sequences not present in observed populations.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: However, MPRAs do not reflect native chromatin context and have been performed in a relatively small number of cell lines, while CRISPR-based experiments are currently limited in throughput.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.65\linewidth]{Figure_4.png} \caption{Three common types of evaluations that can be performed using eQTL data. eQTL data have been used extensively to evaluate variant effect predictions from genomic deep learning models, and different prediction tasks test different aspects of a model's understanding of regulatory variation. eQTL identification evaluates whether a model has learned to identify functionally important regulatory regions or bases in the genome, while eQTL direction prediction evaluates whether a model has learned the direction of effect of functional variants and eQTL effect size prediction evaluates whether a model has learned the magnitude (or magnitude and direction) of effect of functional variants.} \label{fig:eqtl_tasks}
elements.sent-2411-076: Molecular QTL studies \cite{Aguet2023 map associations between observed genetic variants and quantitative molecular traits such as gene expression levels.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Summary statistics from expression QTL (eQTL) studies \cite{Lappalainen2013, Gtex2020 have been used to evaluate variant effect predictions from genomic deep learning models, following three common evaluation frameworks: (1) eQTL identification, (2) eQTL direction prediction, and (3) eQTL effect size prediction (Fig. \ref{fig:eqtl_tasks ).}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Although numerous studies have performed such evaluations, the use of different eQTL datasets and different choices for test set construction make direct performance comparisons difficult.. eQTL identification.
elements.sent-2411-079: This type of task evaluates whether model predictions can distinguish eQTL variants (significantly associated with expression) from non-eQTL variants.
elements.sent-2411-080: Depending on how the non-eQTLs are selected and matched to the eQTLs, this approach can either test whether a model has learned to identify functionally important regulatory regions in the genome (which tend to be enriched for regulatory variants), or it can test whether a model has learned to identify impactful regulatory variants within regulatory regions.
elements.sent-2411-081: The exact reported performance in any given evaluation also depends on the power of the original eQTL study and the significance thresholds or fine-mapping approaches used to define the positive set eQTLs.
elements.sent-2411-082: Many supervised sequence-to-activity models have been evaluated on this type of task \cite{Zhou2015, quang2016danq, Zeng2017cpgenie, wang2018define, Hoffman2019deepfigv, Avsec2021b, Linder2023, celaj2023bigrna, Lal2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-083: The authors of Enformer \cite{Avsec2021b and Borzoi \cite{Linder2023 stratified their eQTL classification tasks by distance to the transcription start site (TSS) and showed that the tested models---Borzoi, Enformer, and Basenji2 \cite{Kelley2020 ---all have much stronger performance for eQTLs close to the TSS than for distal eQTLs, where there is substantial room for improvement.}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: The authors of Akita \cite{Fudenberg2020 and DeepC \cite{Schwessinger2020 , which predict 3D chromatin structure, both showed that their models also have moderate ability to classify eQTLs.}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Similarly, \citet{schiff2024caduceus found that while embeddings from their self-supervised model, Caduceus, result in slightly better predictions than those of NT and HyenaDNA \cite{Nguyen2023 on an eQTL classification task, they do not consistently outperform Enformer embeddings (being slightly better for the most distal eQTLs), with substantial room for improvement for all tested models.. eQTL direction prediction.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: This type of task evaluates whether a model can predict whether a significant eQTL variant increases or decreases gene expression.
elements.sent-2411-087: The exact reported performance will depend on the significance thresholds or fine-mapping approaches used to select eQTLs to include in the evaluation, but does not depend on selecting a matched non-eQTL set.
elements.sent-2411-088: They found that both models struggle to predict the direction of effect of distal eQTLs and have moderate performance for eQTLs within 1kb of the TSS.
elements.sent-2411-089: Using the same dataset, \citet{bajwa2024characterizing found that five replicates of the Basenji2 model make inconsistent direction predictions for more than 50\% of the fine-mapped eQTLs, suggesting high uncertainty in predictions for this task.}
elements.sent-2411-090: This type of task evaluates whether a model can predict the effect size of an eQTL variant.
elements.sent-2411-091: When signed effect sizes are used, this approach tests whether a model has learned both the magnitude and direction of effect.
elements.sent-2411-092: Many supervised models have been evaluated on variations of this task \cite{Kelley2018, Avsec2021b, Karollus2023, Chen2022, Linder2023, Lal2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-093: For example, \citet{Chen2022 evaluated their model, Sei, and found low but positive Spearman correlations between predicted variant effects and signed eQTL effect sizes, which were improved by restricting to fine-mapped eQTLs.}
elements.sent-2411-095: Recent work tested the ability of genomic deep learning models to predict variation in gene expression from genetic variation in personal genomes \cite{Sasse2023, Huang2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-096: Expression predictions generated using personal genome sequences \cite{Lappalainen2013, Bennett2018 were compared to measured personal gene expression levels from RNA-seq experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-097: This evaluation strategy circumvents the issue of causal variant identification, a major limitation of QTL-based evaluations, but requires paired whole genome sequencing and transcriptome data from a suitably large cohort.
elements.sent-2411-098: For a model to succeed at personal gene expression prediction, it must recognize which variants within a personal genome modulate gene expression and correctly identify the direction and magnitude of their effects; thus, this strategy indirectly tests each of the three tasks described above.
elements.sent-2411-099: This type of evaluation is also performed with input sequences and predictions centered at the gene start site rather than at each individual variant (see Section \ref{sec:makingVEPs ), which requires predicting distal variant effects.}
elements.sent-2411-100: Both studies found that the supervised sequence-to-expression models they tested have poor performance when predicting inter-individual variation in gene expression for most genes and sometimes predict the opposite direction of effect, highlighting a major limitation of current models \cite{Sasse2023, Huang2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-101: Follow-up work \cite{Drusinsky2024,Rastogi2024 found that fine-tuning existing models on paired personal genome and transcriptome data leads to some improvement on this task, with performance comparable to linear baselines \cite{Gamazon2015, gusev2016integrative ; however, this strategy does not yet generalize to genes that are unseen during fine-tuning, which suggests that it also does not generalize to unseen (rare or de novo) variants.}}
elements.sent-2411-102: Parallel work indicates that personal genome training may improve prediction of local variant effects from local-context transcription initiation models \cite{he2024training .}
elements.subsubsection-2411-002: Engineered sequence variation (synthetic and perturbation assays)
elements.sent-2411-103: Experimental assays that measure expression from synthetic or perturbed DNA sequences have also been used to evaluate variant effect predictions from genomic deep learning models.. MPRA data.
elements.sent-2411-104: MPRAs are a parallelization of the typical reporter assay, allowing measurement of the functional activity of multiple candidate regulatory sequences in a single experiment.
elements.sent-2411-105: MPRA data \cite{Tewhey2016, WeingartenGabbay2019, Kircher2019, Bergman2022 are increasingly used to evaluate genomic deep learning models, including variant effect predictions \cite{Zhou2018, Hoffman2019deepfigv, minnoye2020cross, Avsec2021b, Toneyan2022, Karollus2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-106: Using an MPRA dataset measuring variant effects on expression through single base-pair saturation mutagenesis of 15 different enhancer and promoter elements \cite{Kircher2019 , \citet{Avsec2021b found that predictions from their Enformer model are more correlated with the MPRA-measured variant effects than predictions from previous models, but with highly variable performance across the different promoters and enhancers.}}
elements.sent-2411-107: The correlations were also substantially lower than the experimental replicate correlations.
elements.sent-2411-108: Both studies also noted that Enformer’s predicted variant effects on accessibility (DNase-seq tracks) perform better than the predicted effects on expression (CAGE-seq tracks) in some cases.. CRISPR-based perturbation experiments.
elements.sent-2411-109: Data from CRISPR-based assays, primarily CRISPRi (CRISPR interference), in which regulatory activity from targeted sequences is suppressed, have been used to evaluate genomic deep learning models on their ability to predict the effects of perturbations to regulatory elements (rather than single nucleotide variants) in a native chromatin context.
elements.sent-2411-110: They found that Enformer can classify experimentally-validated enhancer-gene pairs more accurately than Basenji2, but that both models are more accurate for proximal enhancers than for distal enhancers, in line with the TSS-stratified eQTL results discussed above.
elements.sent-2411-111: For the promoter edits, they found moderate correlation between experimental measurements and predictions from the tested models, with somewhat better performance when using predicted effects on accessibility rather than expression.
elements.sent-2411-112: Interestingly, for the distal enhancer edits, correlation was very low for predictions made at the TSS but moderate for predictions made at the enhancer itself, and the highest accuracy was obtained when accessibility predictions at the enhancer were scaled by the experimentally-measured effect of the entire enhancer on expression.
elements.sent-2411-113: As the efficiency, resolution, and throughput of CRISPR base editing technology improves, we expect that these assays will provide a rich source of ground truth data to more thoroughly benchmark variant effect predictions from genomic deep learning models.
elements.subsection-2411-003: Predicting variant effects on post-transcriptional modifications
elements.sent-2411-114: Many disease-associated variants act by altering post-transcriptional regulatory mechanisms, such as RNA splicing and polyadenylation.
elements.sent-2411-115: Several genomic deep learning models, often designed specifically for these phenotypes, have been evaluated on their ability to predict post-transcriptional variant effects.. Splicing.
elements.sent-2411-116: Several supervised models that predict RNA splicing patterns from DNA sequence have been tested on their ability to predict variant effects on splicing \cite{Jaganathan2019, Zeng2022, Linder2023, Chen2024, Cheng2019, Cheng2021, celaj2023bigrna .}
elements.sent-2411-117: They also noted that the model’s specificity is lower for variants in deep intronic regions than near exons, and that cryptic splice variants with weak or intermediate predictions are more likely to have tissue-specific effects in the experimental data.
elements.sent-2411-118: The average rank prediction of both models also consistently outperforms either model alone.
elements.sent-2411-119: Several supervised models that predict usage of polyadenylation signals (PASs) have been evaluated on variant effect prediction.
elements.sent-2411-120: They found that the performance of all models drops substantially for variants outside of the core hexamer polyadenylation motif, though APARENT2 outperforms the others.
elements.sent-2411-121: Borzoi gives the largest improvement when interpreting variants at larger distances from the PAS, but the performance of all models still drops with increasing distance.
elements.sent-2411-122: At short distances ($<$100bp), the average rank prediction from Borzoi, APARENT2 and Saluki outperforms any individual model.
elements.subsection-2411-004: Predicting variant effects on trait and disease
elements.sent-2411-123: Genomic deep learning models can be used to predict variant effects on organism-level traits and diseases, despite the lack of such information used during training.
elements.sent-2411-124: For models supervised on molecular-level training data, the assumption is that organism-level variant effects are mediated by molecular effects.
elements.sent-2411-125: However, such models do not make predictions about all possible trait-relevant molecular effects, cell types, or cell states.
elements.sent-2411-126: For genomic language models, the assumption is that a disease-causing or trait-altering allele is less likely than a benign allele to appear within the context of its surrounding sequence.
elements.sent-2411-127: This is likely the case for large effect size variants in critical genes or regulatory elements, but less likely for variants that modulate molecular phenotypes with small effect sizes.
elements.sent-2411-128: Data used to evaluate organism-level variant effect predictions are typically obtained either from disease-relevant variant databases, such as ClinVar \cite{Landrum2017 or the Human Gene Mutation Database (HGMD) \cite{Stenson2014 , which primarily contain rare variants with large impact on disease, or from GWAS, which primarily identify disease-associated common variants with small effect sizes.}}
elements.sent-2411-129: Together, these evaluations provide complementary information on a model's ability to predict organism-level variant effects.. Disease variant databases.
elements.sent-2411-130: Databases such as ClinVar \cite{Landrum2017 and HGMD \cite{Stenson2014 aggregate information about known or suspected disease-causing variants with large effect size and high penetrance, which tend to be skewed towards coding variants.}}
elements.sent-2411-131: Non-coding variants in these databases are also skewed towards high effect-size elements, such as translation initiation sequences and splice sites.
elements.sent-2411-132: Other supervised models have been evaluated on ClinVar variants \cite{Hoffman2019deepfigv, Cheng2019, Zeng2022, Leung2018, celaj2023bigrna , with strong performance in certain cases, such as classifying variants located close to splice sites \cite{Cheng2019, Zeng2022 , PASs \cite{Leung2018 , or within UTRs \cite{celaj2023bigrna .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-133: We note that because the above models were evaluated on different datasets, it is difficult to directly compare their performances, highlighting the need for standardized benchmarking studies.
elements.sent-2411-134: Furthermore, reported evaluations on clinical variant databases sometimes include both coding and non-coding variants, making it difficult to disentangle performance on these two classes of variants.. GWAS variants.
elements.sent-2411-135: GWAS summary statistics for complex traits and diseases can be used to evaluate models on their ability to predict whether common variants affect organism-level phenotypes, typically with small effect sizes.
elements.sent-2411-136: As with QTL data, these observational data are complicated by the presence of non-causal associations due to LD.
elements.sent-2411-137: These results suggest that genomic deep learning model predictions are informative for variant effects on organismal traits, although with substantial room for improvement.
elements.sent-2411-138: However, \citet{Dey2020 provided an alternate perspective by testing the informativeness of DeepSEA and Basenji variant effect predictions for trait heritability using stratified LD-score regression \cite{Finucane2015 , conditioned on existing coding, conservation, regulatory element, and LD-related annotations.}}
elements.sent-2411-139: They found that variant effect predictions from DeepSEA and Basenji provide little information beyond these existing annotations, despite being highly enriched for trait heritability.
elements.sent-2411-140: Concurrent with ongoing model development, other studies have leveraged existing genomic deep learning models for various downstream variant effect prediction tasks, summarized below.. Functionally-informed fine-mapping.
elements.sent-2411-141: Separating causal variants from non-causal correlations due to LD is a major challenge for association-based studies.
elements.sent-2411-142: Statistical fine-mapping methods aim to identify likely causal variants as well as credible sets of variants with high likelihood of containing the causal variant within a correlated block \cite{schaid2018genome .}
elements.sent-2411-143: However, credible sets often contain a large number of variants, and approaches to incorporate functional information, such as regulatory element annotations, as probabilistic priors can reduce credible set size \cite{kichaev2014integrating, schaid2018genome .}
elements.sent-2411-144: Using deep learning variant effect predictions as additional annotations to fine-map causal QTL and GWAS variants is a promising application area.
elements.sent-2411-145: In one study, \citet{Wang2021 incorporated Basenji variant effect predictions into a score reflecting a variant’s probability of affecting gene expression and used this score to improve functionally-informed eQTL fine-mapping.}
elements.sent-2411-146: A few other studies have also used deep learning variant effect predictions to prioritize candidate causal variants from association studies \cite{Zhou2018, Schwessinger2020, Hudaiberdiev2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-148: Approximately 74 de novo single nucleotide variants arise each generation \cite{veltman2012novo , a majority in non-coding regions, and identifying which are disease-relevant is challenging.}
elements.sent-2411-149: Genomic deep learning models have been used to prioritize de novo mutations in individuals with Autism Spectrum Disorder \cite{Zhou2019, Jaganathan2019, Trevino2021, Linder2022 and congenital heart disease \cite{Richter2020, Ameen2022 based on their predicted functional impact.}}
elements.sent-2411-150: In some cases, the prioritized variants were experimentally validated \cite{Zhou2019, Jaganathan2019, Ameen2022 , supporting the utility of model-based de novo variant prioritization.}
elements.sent-2411-151: Mechanistic hypothesis generation in a disease context.
elements.sent-2411-152: Genomic deep learning model predictions, along with model interpretability techniques \cite{Shrikumar2017, Lundberg2017, simonyan2013inputgradient, Novakovsky2022 , can also be used to gain insight into disease mechanisms, such as identifying the cell types, genes, and regulatory elements involved.}
elements.sent-2411-153: Several recent studies trained models on chromatin accessibility data from cell types or tissues relevant to a particular disease \cite{Wang2021a, Trevino2021, Ameen2022, loeb2024variants and predicted the specific cell types and transcription factor motifs most likely affected by disease-relevant variants.}
elements.sent-2411-154: Other studies have also used deep learning variant effect predictions for mechanistic interpretation of variants from GWAS or clinical databases \cite{Zhou2018, Chen2022, Yang2022, Alipanahi2015, Kim2021, celaj2023bigrna .}
elements.sent-2411-155: These studies provide a blueprint for how genomic deep learning approaches can be used to generate hypotheses about the cellular mechanisms mediating complex diseases.
elements.sent-2411-156: In this Review, we discuss emerging trends in using sequence-based genomic deep learning models for non-coding variant effect prediction.
elements.sent-2411-157: Supervised sequence-to-activity models can identify and provide mechanistic interpretations of regulatory variants that impact a range of phenotypes, including chromatin states, gene expression, post-transcriptional modifications, and complex traits.
elements.sent-2411-158: Current models have highest performance on high effect size variants and on local effects close to the site of a variant, while they struggle with predicting distal variant effects.
elements.sent-2411-159: In general, there is substantial room for improvement on variant interpretation tasks, and model design choices that are optimal for performance on the original training tasks may not always be optimal for variant effect prediction \cite{Kathail2024 , highlighting the importance of considering variant-based evaluations during model development.}
elements.sent-2411-160: Self-supervised genomic language models have been less comprehensively evaluated on non-coding variant effect prediction tasks than supervised models, but achieve similar performance on some tasks, particularly when fine-tuned.
elements.sent-2411-161: The utility of current self-supervised models relative to supervised models remains unclear in independent benchmarking studies \cite{Tang2024, marin2024bend, kao2024lrbenchmark , indicating that additional work is needed to make optimal use of self-supervised tasks in genomic applications.}
elements.sent-2411-162: Self-supervised pre-training has been most successful in domains with vast amounts of unlabeled data and smaller amounts of labeled data, whereas the human genome has a fixed size and genome-wide functional data are available for many important molecular phenotypes.
elements.sent-2411-163: Leveraging genomes from multiple species \cite{Dalla-Torre2023, Benegas2023a can increase the amount of unlabeled data available, though sequence conservation between species limits these gains.}
elements.sent-2411-164: For both supervised and self-supervised approaches, models are fundamentally limited by the amount of sequence diversity available for training, which may be particularly relevant for their ability to understand the effects of small sequence variations.
elements.sent-2411-165: High-throughput profiling of diverse synthetic DNA sequences has been proposed as an additional source of training data \cite{deBoer2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-166: Several studies have trained deep learning models on MPRA data, primarily in the context of sequence design \cite{movva2019deciphering, de2022deepstarr, reddy2023pretraining, gosai2023machine , and additional work is needed to test whether such approaches can predict the effects of naturally occurring, trait-relevant genetic variation.}
elements.sent-2411-167: As the scale and throughput of MPRA and CRISPR-based experiments continues to grow, we anticipate that these datasets will become a rich source of training data for genomic deep learning models.
elements.sent-2411-168: In parallel, models trained on single cell data (such as scBasset \cite{Yuan2022 ) and approaches to predict context-specific effects without explicit training data (such as ChromDragoNN \cite{nair2019integrating ) may be effective for predicting context-specific variant effects in cell types for which we have little or no experimental assay data.}}
elements.sent-2411-169: Finally, we highlight the need for standardized variant effect prediction benchmarks and datasets, so that different modeling and variant scoring decisions can be systematically compared.
elements.sent-2411-170: Care is needed to design benchmarks that distinguish between the ability of model predictions to identify functionally important genomic regions, which are enriched for functional variants, and their ability to identify specific functional variants within such regions.
elements.sent-2411-171: In addition, relatively few evaluations have been performed for variant effects on local epigenetic or chromatin phenotypes, which may shed light on model failure modes.
elements.sent-2411-172: For example, current models struggle with predicting directions of variant effects on gene expression, which may either reflect similar difficulties in predicting directions of effect on local regulatory element activity, or reflect difficulties in modeling relationships between regulatory elements and genes.
elements.sent-2411-173: Most existing model evaluations have also focused on single nucleotide variants, but insertions, deletions, and larger structural variants are important contributors to phenotypic variation.
elements.sent-2411-174: Effect prediction for these types of variants requires comparing sequences of different lengths, and robust evaluations and best practices are yet to be established.
elements.sent-2411-175: Overall, sequence-based genomic deep learning models are a promising approach to a variety of downstream variant effect prediction tasks, and future modeling improvements and systematic benchmarking analyses should continue to improve their utility for variant interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-176: We thank members of the Ioannidis lab for helpful discussions, particularly eyes robson, Ruchir Rastogi, and Aniketh Janardhan Reddy for their comments on the manuscript.
elements.sent-2411-177: This work was partially supported by the UC Noyce Initiative for Computational Transformation.. N.M.I.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figure_5.png} \caption{Overview of general types of sequence-to-activity models. a) Models that predict only local phenotypes---such as chromatin accessibility, TF binding, histone modifications, and polyadenylation---typically rely on local sequence context ($<$5,000bp). These models either make predictions about binned data within 100-200bp sequence windows (e.g. whether the window at the center of the input sequence contains a chromatin accessibility peak) or base resolution predictions (e.g. read depth at every position within the input sequence). Either multi-task or single-task predictions can be made. b) Models that predict gene expression aim to capture the effects of both proximal and distal regulatory elements. Some models capture distal sequence context directly by using a larger sequence input size, while other models use a hierarchical approach by combining local chromatin state predictions across many proximal and distal sequence windows. } \label{fig:model_types}
elements.sent-2411-178: Supervised sequence-to-activity models are neural networks trained to map from input DNA sequences to functional genomics outputs.
elements.sent-2411-179: Design choices include the number, size and type of layers in the network (e.g. fully connected, convolutional, or transformer layers), input and convolutional filter sizes, activation functions, and loss functions \cite{Zou2018, Eraslan2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-180: Most supervised models utilize convolutional layers early in the network, due to their suitability for modeling DNA sequence motifs \cite{Zou2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-181: Design choices are also informed by the prediction task(s), whether they are regression or classification, and how much sequence context they require.
elements.sent-2411-182: Including more sequence context (the length of input DNA sequence considered by a model) makes it possible to learn distal regulatory effects but can also be computationally prohibitive.. Local sequence models.
elements.sent-2411-183: Many models use short input sequences ($<$5,000bp; Fig. \ref{fig:model_types a) to make predictions about locally-regulated phenotypes, such as chromatin accessibility, TF binding, histone modifications, DNA methylation, transcription initiation, polyadenylation, or splicing, using either single-task or multi-task learning \cite{Alipanahi2015, Zhou2015, quang2016danq, Kelley2016, Zeng2017cpgenie, wang2018define, Leung2018, Hoffman2019deepfigv, Cheng2019, Maslova2020, minnoye2020cross, Avsec2021a, Cheng2021, Chen2022, Linder2022, PampariChrombpnet, Dudnyk2024, he2024dissection, Cochran2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-184: Early examples include DeepSEA \cite{Zhou2015 and Basset \cite{Kelley2016 , which use convolutional, pooling, and fully connected layers to predict functional activity in multiple cell types.}}
elements.sent-2411-185: Pooling layers reduce the dimension of the data representations, enabling models to efficiently handle longer input sequences but also reducing the resolution of the output predictions.
elements.sent-2411-186: An alternative approach, used by BPNet \cite{Avsec2021a and others \cite{PampariChrombpnet, Linder2022, he2024dissection, Dudnyk2024, Cochran2024 , involves forgoing or minimizing pooling layers to achieve base resolution prediction.. Distal sequence models.}}
elements.sent-2411-187: Models that predict gene expression often incorporate a wider sequence context by directly expanding the input sequence length (Fig. \ref{fig:model_types b, left) \cite{Kelley2018, Kelley2020, Agarwal2020, Avsec2021b, Linder2023, celaj2023bigrna .}}
elements.sent-2411-188: Basenji \cite{Kelley2018, Kelley2020 utilizes dilated convolutions (containing gaps between the convolutional filter values to scan longer inputs without increasing parameter count) to achieve a 32kb sequence context.}
elements.sent-2411-189: Basenji's successor, Enformer \cite{Avsec2021b , incorporates a transformer architecture \cite{Vaswani2017 to increase the sequence context to $\sim$200kb.}}
elements.sent-2411-190: The transformer's self-attention mechanism, originally developed for modeling natural language, enables learning of relevant features from distal regions within an input sequence.
elements.sent-2411-191: Another successor, Borzoi \cite{Linder2023 , includes a U-net architecture \cite{Ronneberger2015 to enable higher resolution predictions (32bp, versus 128bp for Basenji and Enformer) and an expanded sequence context of $\sim$500kb.}}
elements.sent-2411-192: Distal sequence models have also been developed to predict other phenotypes, including 3D genome folding (Akita \cite{Fudenberg2020 , DeepC \cite{Schwessinger2020 , Orca \cite{Zhou2022 ), mRNA splicing (SpliceAI \cite{Jaganathan2019 , Pangolin \cite{Zeng2022 ), and transcription initiation (Puffin-D \cite{Dudnyk2024 ).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-193: While the largest current models have reached context sizes of hundreds of kilobases, they may still not capture the full biological context relevant to a given task, such as very distal enhancers.
elements.sent-2411-194: Furthermore, they often struggle to fully utilize distal features within their own input contexts \cite{Karollus2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-195: Accurately modeling information from long input contexts remains an active area of broader deep learning research \cite{Liu2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-197: Rather than directly modeling long input sequences, ExPecto \cite{Zhou2018 processes longer sequences by first making epigenetic and chromatin state predictions on shorter sub-sequences and then using these predictions as inputs to a secondary model of gene expression (Fig. \ref{fig:model_types b, right).}}
elements.sent-2411-198: This hierarchical approach is motivated by the biology of gene expression and may be an effective method for integrating distal information, but exploration of modeling choices---particularly for the chromatin-to-expression stage---has so far been limited.
elements.sent-2411-199: While supervised architectures provide a powerful approach to learn salient sequence patterns from functional genomics data, they require training data from cell types of interest and do not generalize to unseen assays or cell types without additional training or fine-tuning (Box 3).
elements.sent-2411-200: Genomic language modeling is a relatively new paradigm, and many such models trained on human or cross-species genomes have been recently proposed \cite{Zaheer2020, Ji2021, Yang2022, Zhou2023, Dalla-Torre2023, Benegas2023a, Nguyen2023, Fishman2023, Gndz2023, robson2023guanine, sanabria2023grover, zhang2023dnagpt, schiff2024caduceus, Chen2024, chu2024utrLM, zhu2024CDGPT, McNally2024allspice , along with models trained on smaller genomes, such as Arabidopsis thaliana \cite{Benegas2023 , fungal \cite{Karollus2024 , or prokaryotic \cite{nguyen2024sequence genomes.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-201: By learning the statistical distributions present in natural genome sequences using self-supervised pre-training tasks derived from the sequences themselves, genomic language models are thought to synthesize salient representations of sequence features, known as embeddings, during pre-training.
elements.sent-2411-202: Direct use of the resulting sequence embeddings (zero-shot prediction) may yield good performance on certain sequence-based prediction tasks \cite{Benegas2023, Benegas2023a, Dalla-Torre2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-203: More commonly, a pre-trained model is used as an informative prior or initialization of model parameters, and the model is further trained on supervised prediction tasks using task-specific datasets (e.g. functional genomics data), requiring less data than would be required for supervised training from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-204: This additional supervised training, also called fine-tuning (\ref{sec:box3 ), allows a pre-trained model to learn to produce outputs tailored to the desired downstream task \cite{Ji2021, Dalla-Torre2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-205: The difficulty of obtaining large training datasets for many tasks of interest makes the paradigm of pre-training followed by fine-tuning particularly promising.. Pre-training tasks.
elements.sent-2411-206: Most genomic language models process an input DNA sequence into short subsequences (tokens, akin to ``words'') using tokenization schemes such as k-mer tokenization or byte-pair encoding.
elements.sent-2411-207: Following approaches designed for natural language processing, genomic language models typically use pre-training tasks such as masked language modeling (MLM), in which a subset of tokens (often 15\% by convention) are masked during pre-training and learned from surrounding context, or autoregressive language modeling (ALM), in which a subsequent token is learned from previous context.
elements.sent-2411-208: Conceptually, MLM is a ``fill-in-the blank'' approach, while ALM is predicting the next token.
elements.sent-2411-209: In natural language processing, perplexity per token---a measure of the probability or uncertainty assigned by the model to unseen tokens---is an intrinsic, cross-model metric that can be used to compare models trained with different pre-training tasks, but this practice has not yet been widely adopted in genomics.
elements.sent-2411-210: As with supervised models, language model architectures vary.
elements.sent-2411-211: While the self-attention mechanism of the transformer architecture \cite{Vaswani2017 is central to many models, the paradigm of self-supervised pre-training is not limited to transformers, and some genomic language models have utilized different mechanisms \cite{Zaheer2020, Benegas2023a, Poli2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-212: Encoder-decoder architectures, which learn input representations via an encoder neural network and reconstructions of inputs from those representations via a decoder neural network, are common in language modeling.
elements.sent-2411-213: Currently, most genomic language models are encoder-only models trained with MLM \cite{consens2023transformers .}
elements.sent-2411-214: Several self-supervised genomic language models have been evaluated on human non-coding variant effect prediction tasks (Table 1), as discussed in Section \ref{sec:evalVEP .}
elements.sent-2411-215: We anticipate that genomic language models will need more systematic evaluation of their zero-shot and fine-tuned performance on variants, including extensive direct comparisons to supervised models, before they are widely adopted in downstream variant effect prediction applications.
elements.sent-2411-216: Probing and fine-tuning are two common approaches for supervised training of a pre-trained model on a new task of interest.
elements.sent-2411-217: Probing typically refers to linear probing \cite{Alain2017 , in which the pre-trained model parameters are unchanged but a new linear layer is learned from the resulting embeddings to predict task-specific data (Fig. \ref{fig:probing_finetuning a).}}
elements.sent-2411-218: Fine-tuning typically involves replacing the pre-trained model outputs with outputs for a new prediction task and allowing the pre-trained model parameters to be updated while performing additional training using a task-relevant loss function and a new type of data (Fig. \ref{fig:probing_finetuning b).}
elements.sent-2411-219: After fine-tuning a genomic language model, variant effect predictions can be extracted using the same approaches as described in the main text for supervised models.
elements.sent-2411-220: While probing and fine-tuning are particularly important concepts for genomic language models, the notion of additional supervised training on a new prediction task (e.g. a variant effect prediction task) is also applicable to supervised models.
elements.sent-2411-221: An important advantage of probing and fine-tuning is that they require less data for the new prediction task than would be required to train on that task from scratch, since the pre-trained model serves as an informative prior on model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-222: Both probing and fine-tuning are related to the notion of transfer learning---in which a model trained on one task is further trained or utilized for another task---which is often beneficial in cases where the tasks are related to each other.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figure_6.png} \caption{Probing and fine-tuning. Two common paradigms for further training of an existing (pre-trained) model on a new task of interest are shown. a) Probing is done by training an additional linear layer to predict task-specific outputs from the embeddings of a fixed-parameter model that was previously trained on other tasks. b) Fine-tuning, in contrast to probing, allows parameter updates to the original model as well as to the new linear layer or task head, resulting in shifting embeddings as well as output predictions.} \label{fig:probing_finetuning}
